{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and supplemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials aqqeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the aqqointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and suqqlemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Aqqointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for aqqointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for aqqointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials aqqeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the aqqointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and suqqlemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Aqqointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for aqqointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials aqqeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the aqqointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and suqqlemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Aqqointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for aqqointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

* '''Concurrent Official Aqqointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for aqqointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials aqqeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the aqqointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and suqqlemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Aqqointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for aqqointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials a11eared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the a11ointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official A11ointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for a11ointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

[[Category:Senji]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Senji}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] <!--Senji-->[[category:Senji]] [[Category:Senji]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Senji}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] <!--Senji-->[[Category:Senji]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Senji}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] <!--Senji-->[[category:Senji]] [[Category:Senji]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Senji}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Senji]][[Category:11]] <!--Senji-->[[Category:11]][[category:Senji]][[Category:11]]

{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common.== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

revision.text:
{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

{{otheruses|{{ill|Imperial edicts and official documents|ja|天皇}} or oral orders by aristocrats|The title of a senior female servant or {{ill|nyōbō|ja|女房}}|{{ill|Senji (official position)|ja|宣旨 (役職)}}}} '''Senji''' (宣旨) refers to the format of documents used to transmit the orders of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} or the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} in post-{{ill|Ritsuryō period|ja|律令期}} Japan. It is one of the document forms issued by the imperial court, a variant of {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}}.
== Overview == The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}'s orders or intentions ({{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}) were transcribed into documents such as {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkanchō|ja|太政官牒}} by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}}, with {{ill|Shi (Ritsuryō)|ja|史 (律令制)}} staff receiving the emperor's commands orally. To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.

While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

To avoid forgetting the orders, scribes created personal notes, which gradually evolved into formalized documents called Senji. While only the signatures of the scribe and other officials appeared on the documents, they were recognized as reflecting the emperor’s will. This conferred authority on unsigned documents, occasionally leading to the issuance of {{ill|Fake imperial edicts|ja|偽勅|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|12=draft}} period.
Initially, imperial commands were issued as {{ill|Imperial edicts|ja|詔書|12=draft}} or {{ill|Chokushi|ja|勅旨|12=draft}}, which were reserved for significant matters and strictly regulated in form. To allow for greater flexibility, the Senji format was introduced.
=== Issuance Procedure === The issuance procedure involved conveying the emperor's command through the {{ill|Naishi no Tsukasa|ja|内侍司|12=draft}}, followed by the {{ill|Kurododokoro|ja|蔵人頭|12=draft}}, and then to the appointed {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|上卿|12=draft}}. The Jōkyō would transmit it to relevant offices, including the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局|12=draft}}, {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Naiki-kyoku|ja|内記局|12=draft}}, which would draft the Senji. This process began during the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (810–824). Commands passed from the Naishi to the Kurododokoro were called {{ill|Naishi-sen|ja|内侍宣|12=draft}} and later evolved into {{ill|Nyōbō Hōsho|ja|女房奉書|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}. Oral orders from the Kurododokoro to the Jōkyō, known as {{ill|Kusenshō|ja|口宣|12=draft}}, were later documented and su11lemented with {{ill|Kusenshō-an|ja|口宣案|12=draft}} as drafts.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, Senji issued by the {{ill|Geki-kyoku|ja|外記局|12=draft}} in their name were recognized. Additionally, documents issued by the Benkankyoku on the order of high-ranking officials ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō|12=draft}}) instead of the {{ill|Daijōkanpu|ja|太政官符|12=draft}} were called {{ill|Kan-senji|ja|官宣旨|12=draft}}.
=== Examples of Senji === Examples of Senji include:
* '''Concurrent Official Appointment (Yamakai-ki)''': 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記 (Translation): Minister of the Interior Fujiwara no Ason declares: By imperial decree, the aforementioned person is to continue serving concurrently as Ukon no Dainagon. February 11, 1167, by the Great External Scribe.
* '''Acting Regent Senji (Koryū-ki)''': 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉 (Translation): Senior Second Rank Acting Chancellor and Minister to the Dowager Empress' Palace Fujiwara no Ason Kintō declares: By imperial decree, the Left Minister shall perform the duties of a regent for appointments and miscellaneous matters. October 27, 1015, by the Great External Scribe Ono no Ason Fumiyoshi.
=== Types of Senji === {{section-stub}}
== Insei and Rinji == {{main|{{ill|Insen|ja|院宣|12=draft}}|{{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨|12=draft}}}} During the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|12=draft}} period and the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, the issuance of {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣|12=draft}} exceeded Senji. Additionally, {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨|12=draft}}, a simplified form of Senji, became more common. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial decrees|ja|詔勅|12=draft}} * {{ill|Insen|ja|院宣|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rinji|ja|綸旨|12=draft}} * {{ill|Reijishi|ja|令旨|12=draft}} * {{ill|Gokyōsho|ja|御教書|12=draft}} * {{ill|Gonaisho|ja|御内書|12=draft}} * {{ill|Uda Insenji|ja|宇多院宣旨|12=draft}} * {{ill|Juei 2nd Year Tenth Month Senji|ja|寿永二年十月宣旨|12=draft}} * {{ill|Juei 3rd Year Senji|ja|寿永三年宣旨|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kobunsho|ja|古文書|12=draft}}
== Further Reading == * Shohachi Hayakawa, *Senji Shiron*, Iwanami Shoten, April 1990, ISBN 4-00-001193-6.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

38 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:宣旨 up to rev 94110590
revision.text:
{{otheruses|[[天皇]]など貴人の口頭命令および公文書|上級女性使用人である[[女房]]の筆頭|宣旨 (役職)}} '''宣旨'''（せんじ）は、律令期以降の日本において[[天皇]]・[[太政官]]の命令を伝達する文書の形式名。朝廷が出す文書の形態の一つ。[[詔勅]]の変体。
== 概略 == [[天皇]]の命令・意向（[[勅旨]]）が太政官において[[太政官符]]・[[太政官牒]]などとして文書化される際、文書作成を行う[[弁官局]]の[[史 (律令制)|史]]が口頭で命令・意向を受ける。このとき、弁官史は、命令・意向の内容を忘れないために自らのメモを作成した。このメモが、当事者へ発給されるようになり、文書として様式化していき宣旨となった。文書には、弁官・史などの署名しか記されなかったが、天皇の意を反映した文書として認識され、取り扱われた。[[印璽]]なき文章に権威が付加されることになり、[[幕末]]にはしばしば[[偽勅]]が発せられることになった。
本来、[[律令]]に規定されている天皇発給の命令書としては、[[詔書]]・[[勅旨]]があったが、これらの発給は重要な案件の場合に限定されており、また形式が厳密に定められており、特定の機会にしか発給ができなかった。そこで、柔軟に発給可能な宣旨という文書形態が登場したのである。
=== 発給手続 === 発給手続きは、[[天皇]]が[[内侍司|内侍]]に伝え、そこから[[蔵人頭]]に、蔵人頭から担当[[上卿]]に上卿から[[外記局]]、[[弁官]]、[[内記局]]などに伝えられて初めて発給された。[[弘仁]]年間頃から始められた。内侍から蔵人頭に伝えられる文書は「[[内侍宣]]｣と呼ばれ、[[鎌倉時代]]以降に｢[[女房奉書]]｣へと発展する。また、蔵人頭が上卿に伝える時は、口頭が原則で「[[口宣]]｣と呼ばれたが後に文書化され、更に口宣の控えである[[口宣案]]が作成されることもあった。
[[平安時代|平安期]]に入ると、元々詔勅の検討を担当していた外記局が、天皇の命令・意向を自らの名で文書化したものも宣旨と呼ばれるようになった。また、弁官が議政官（[[公卿]]）の命令・意向を受けて、正式な太政官符の代わりに弁官名で発給した文書は[[官宣旨]]（かんせんじ）と呼ばれた。
=== 宣旨の例 === 宣旨の例
○兼官の宣旨（山槐記） 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記
（訓読文） 内大臣藤原朝臣（花山院忠雅 正二位 44歳）正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通（久我 50歳）宣（の）る 勅（みことのり）を奉（うけたまは）るに、件（くだん）の人、宜しく旧（もと）の如く右近（衛）大将を兼任すべし者（てへり） 仁安2年（1167年）2月11日 大外記（清原真人頼業 46歳 六位か?） ※同日付にて藤原忠雅は、大納言兼右近衛大将から内大臣に転任し、この宣旨によって、右近衛大将の兼任を留任する。
○准摂政の宣旨（小右記）
正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉
（訓読文） 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任（四條 50歳）宣る 勅を奉るに、除目等雑事、宜しく左大臣（藤原道長 正二位 50歳）をして摂政に准（なぞら）ひ、儀（こと）之（これ）を行はしむべし者（てへり） 長和4年（1015年）10月27日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉（うけたまは）る
=== 宣旨の種類 === {{節スタブ}}
== 院宣・綸旨 == {{main|院宣|綸旨}} [[院政]]期から[[鎌倉時代|鎌倉期]]以降は、次第に[[院宣]]の発給が宣旨の発給を上回るようになった。
また、宣旨をさらに手続きのうえで簡略化した[[綸旨]]も出されるようになった。
==関連項目== *[[詔勅]] *[[院宣]] *[[綸旨]] *[[令旨]] *[[御教書]] *[[御内書]] *[[宇多院宣旨]] *[[寿永二年十月宣旨]] *[[寿永三年宣旨]] *[[古文書]]
==関連文献== *[[早川庄八]]『宣旨試論』、[[岩波書店]]、[[1990年]][[4月]]、ISBN 4-00-001193-6
{{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:せんし}} [[Category:古文書]] [[Category:宣旨|*]] [[Category:命令文書]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses|[[天皇]]など貴人の口頭命令および公文書|上級女性使用人である[[女房]]の筆頭|宣旨 (役職)}} '''宣旨'''（せんじ）は、律令期以降の日本において[[天皇]]・[[太政官]]の命令を伝達する文書の形式名。朝廷が出す文書の形態の一つ。[[詔勅]]の変体。
== 概略 == [[天皇]]の命令・意向（[[勅旨]]）が太政官において[[太政官符]]・[[太政官牒]]などとして文書化される際、文書作成を行う[[弁官局]]の[[史 (律令制)|史]]が口頭で命令・意向を受ける。このとき、弁官史は、命令・意向の内容を忘れないために自らのメモを作成した。このメモが、当事者へ発給されるようになり、文書として様式化していき宣旨となった。文書には、弁官・史などの署名しか記されなかったが、天皇の意を反映した文書として認識され、取り扱われた。[[印璽]]なき文章に権威が付加されることになり、[[幕末]]にはしばしば[[偽勅]]が発せられることになった。
本来、[[律令]]に規定されている天皇発給の命令書としては、[[詔書]]・[[勅旨]]があったが、これらの発給は重要な案件の場合に限定されており、また形式が厳密に定められており、特定の機会にしか発給ができなかった。そこで、柔軟に発給可能な宣旨という文書形態が登場したのである。
=== 発給手続 === 発給手続きは、[[天皇]]が[[内侍司|内侍]]に伝え、そこから[[蔵人頭]]に、蔵人頭から担当[[上卿]]に上卿から[[外記局]]、[[弁官]]、[[内記局]]などに伝えられて初めて発給された。[[弘仁]]年間頃から始められた。内侍から蔵人頭に伝えられる文書は「[[内侍宣]]｣と呼ばれ、[[鎌倉時代]]以降に｢[[女房奉書]]｣へと発展する。また、蔵人頭が上卿に伝える時は、口頭が原則で「[[口宣]]｣と呼ばれたが後に文書化され、更に口宣の控えである[[口宣案]]が作成されることもあった。
[[平安時代|平安期]]に入ると、元々詔勅の検討を担当していた外記局が、天皇の命令・意向を自らの名で文書化したものも宣旨と呼ばれるようになった。また、弁官が議政官（[[公卿]]）の命令・意向を受けて、正式な太政官符の代わりに弁官名で発給した文書は[[官宣旨]]（かんせんじ）と呼ばれた。
=== 宣旨の例 === 宣旨の例
○兼官の宣旨（山槐記） 内大臣藤原朝臣 正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通宣 奉 勅、件人宜如旧兼任右近大将者 仁安二年二月十一日 大外記
（訓読文） 内大臣藤原朝臣（花山院忠雅 正二位 44歳）正二位行大納言源朝臣雅通（久我 50歳）宣（の）る 勅（みことのり）を奉（うけたまは）るに、件（くだん）の人、宜しく旧（もと）の如く右近（衛）大将を兼任すべし者（てへり） 仁安2年（1167年）2月11日 大外記（清原真人頼業 46歳 六位か?） ※同日付にて藤原忠雅は、大納言兼右近衛大将から内大臣に転任し、この宣旨によって、右近衛大将の兼任を留任する。
○准摂政の宣旨（小右記）
正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任宣 奉 勅、除目等雑事、宜令左大臣准摂政儀行之者 長和四年十月廿七日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉
（訓読文） 正二位行権大納言兼太皇太后宮大夫藤原朝臣公任（四條 50歳）宣る 勅を奉るに、除目等雑事、宜しく左大臣（藤原道長 正二位 50歳）をして摂政に准（なぞら）ひ、儀（こと）之（これ）を行はしむべし者（てへり） 長和4年（1015年）10月27日 大外記小野朝臣文義奉（うけたまは）る
=== 宣旨の種類 === {{節スタブ}}
== 院宣・綸旨 == {{main|院宣|綸旨}} [[院政]]期から[[鎌倉時代|鎌倉期]]以降は、次第に[[院宣]]の発給が宣旨の発給を上回るようになった。
また、宣旨をさらに手続きのうえで簡略化した[[綸旨]]も出されるようになった。
==関連項目== *[[詔勅]] *[[院宣]] *[[綸旨]] *[[令旨]] *[[御教書]] *[[御内書]] *[[宇多院宣旨]] *[[寿永二年十月宣旨]] *[[寿永三年宣旨]] *[[古文書]]
==関連文献== *[[早川庄八]]『宣旨試論』、[[岩波書店]]、[[1990年]][[4月]]、ISBN 4-00-001193-6
{{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:せんし}} [[Category:古文書]] [[Category:宣旨|*]] [[Category:命令文書]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}
